summary: Housekeeping PR that removed ten deprecated Python/tooling files from the repository, deleting 1122 lines with no additions. The deleted tools included old data conversion/postprocessing helpers, map/symbol utilities, directory HTML dumping, an object-file patching script, and mwld patch checksum files. There were no PR body notes, comments, reviews, or review feedback in the provided slice, so the rationale is grounded mainly in the title and the all-deletion diff.
what_changed: Deleted tools/convert_data.py, a 314-line script that scanned .4byte/NULL assembly data and tried to rewrite runs as asciz strings, floats, doubles, signed/unsigned ints, or raw hex words.
Deleted tools/postprocess.py, the largest removed file at 348 lines; exact contents are not included in the excerpt, but it was one of the deprecated Python tools removed by this PR.

Deleted tools/frank.py, a 223-line object-file/profile-code manipulation script that patched PowerPC instruction byte sequences, stripped profiling extra bytes, handled branch-to-link-register sequences, and performed instruction reordering around epilogues.
Deleted tools/parse_map.py, tools/dump_tree.py, tools/rename_symbols.py, and tools/merge_double.py as deprecated helper scripts.
Deleted tools/mwld_patch.py plus tools/mwld_prepatch.sha1 and tools/mwld_postpatch.sha1, removing the remaining small mwld patch artifacts/checksum files.
The PR made no source, header, or matching-code additions; it was purely removal of obsolete tooling.
smart_moves: Removed entire deprecated tools rather than leaving stale scripts in place, reducing maintenance surface and the risk that future contributors rely on unsupported workflows.
Grouped related obsolete Python utilities and mwld patch artifacts into one focused housekeeping change, making the review scope straightforward.
Kept the change mechanically simple: deleted files only, with zero added lines, minimizing risk of introducing behavioral changes in the decompilation codebase itself.
decomp_lessons: Decomp repositories accumulate one-off conversion, postprocessing, map parsing, and binary patch helpers; once workflows move on, removing deprecated scripts helps keep current matching procedures discoverable.
Tools that rewrite assembly data heuristically, such as converting .4byte runs into asciz/float/int directives, can become misleading if they are no longer part of the blessed workflow.

Old object patching utilities like frank.py may encode fragile assumptions about compiler output, profiling bytes, instruction scheduling, or linker behavior; if deprecated, preserving them in the active tools directory can confuse future matching/debugging efforts.

When deleting tooling, include enough evidence in the PR title or body to indicate deprecation status. This slice has no body/comments, so future readers must infer rationale almost entirely from the title.
naming_conventions: Deleted tooling used lowercase snake_case Python script names under tools/, such as convert_data.py, dump_tree.py, merge_double.py, parse_map.py, rename_symbols.py, mwld_patch.py, and postprocess.py.
mwld patch checksum artifacts followed the names mwld_prepatch.sha1 and mwld_postpatch.sha1.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: Removed convert_data.py, which had supported heuristic assembly data cleanup by detecting zero-terminated ASCII strings, f32/f64 values, signed/unsigned 32-bit integers, NULL dwords, and .balign 4 padding from .4byte sequences.

Removed frank.py, which had implemented binary/object-level matching tactics for profile/vanilla object reconciliation: stripping PROFILE_EXTRA_BYTES, fixing branch-to-link-register byte sequences, preserving vanilla code when profile code size differed, and reordering lwz/li/lfs/fdivs/lmw/lfd-style instruction patterns around epilogues.
Because these tactics were deleted as deprecated tools, future work should not assume they are current project-approved workflows without checking newer tooling or documentation.
review_feedback: No issue comments, review comments, reviews, or PR body text were present in the provided slice.
No explicit reviewer guidance or requested changes were available.
follow_up_queries: Which current tools replaced convert_data.py and postprocess.py for assembly data cleanup?
Was frank.py superseded by another object/profile patching workflow, or was the profiling approach abandoned?
Are any documentation pages or build scripts still referencing the deleted tools?
When removing deprecated tooling in future PRs, include a short body listing the replacement workflow or reason for deprecation.
